file_name: SightLife Surgical, Inc. - STRATEGIC SALES _ MARKETING AGREEMENT.txt

perturbation.original_text: Surgical shall perform the Services in compliance with all applicable federal, state and local laws and regulations.
perturbation.changed_text: Surgical shall endeavor to perform the Services in general accordance with applicable federal, state, and local laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing "in compliance with" with "endeavor to perform in general accordance with" creates ambiguity. It suggests an attempt to comply rather than strict compliance, making the obligation less enforceable. The term "general accordance" is vague and open to interpretation, weakening the requirement to adhere strictly to all laws and regulations. This introduces a level of discretion that could lead to non-compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 2234 defines unprofessional conduct for licensed professionals, which includes violations of laws and regulations related to their practice. By suggesting that Surgical only needs to "endeavor" to comply, the modified text contradicts the expectation that licensed professionals (including those involved in pharmaceutical sales and marketing) must strictly adhere to all applicable laws. This weakens the obligation for full compliance, potentially enabling violations of the law.

Enforcement [2220 - 2319] ( Article 12 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1313, Sec. 2. ) 2234. The board shall take action against any licensee who is charged with unprofessional conduct. In addition to other provisions of this article, unprofessional conduct includes, but is not limited to, the following: (a) Violating or attempting to violate, directly or indirectly, assisting in or abetting the violation of, or conspiring to violate any provision of this chapter. (b) Gross negligence. (c) Repeated negligent acts. To be repeated, there must be two or more negligent acts or omissions. An initial negligent act or omission followed by a separate and distinct departure from the applicable standard of care shall constitute repeated negligent acts. (1) An initial negligent diagnosis followed by an act or omission medically appropriate for that negligent diagnosis of the patient shall constitute a single negligent act. (2) When the standard of care requires a change in the diagnosis, act, or omission that constitutes the negligent act described in para

Previous Next 2234. The board shall take action against any licensee who is charged with unprofessional conduct. In addition to other provisions of this article, unprofessional conduct includes, but is not limited to, the following: (a) Violating or attempting to violate, directly or indirectly, assisting in or abetting the violation of, or conspiring to violate any provision of this chapter. (b) Gross negligence. (c) Repeated negligent acts. To be repeated, there must be two or more negligent acts or omissions. An initial negligent act or omission followed by a separate and distinct departure from the applicable standard of care shall constitute repeated negligent acts. (1) An initial negligent diagnosis followed by an act or omission medically appropriate for that negligent diagnosis of the patient shall constitute a single negligent act. (2) When the standard of care requires a change in the diagnosis, act, or omission that constitutes the negligent act described in paragraph (1), including, but not limited to, a reevaluation of the diagnosis or a change in treatment, and the licensee’s conduct departs from the applicable standard of care, each departure constitutes a separate and distinct breach of the standard of care. (d) Incompetence. (e) The commission of any act involving dishonesty or corruption that is substantially related to the qualifications, functions, or duties
perturbation.justification: The modified text, by replacing "in compliance with" to "endeavor to perform in general accordance with", weakens the obligation to strictly adhere to laws and regulations. This creates ambiguity and introduces discretion, potentially leading to non-compliance. California Business and Professions Code § 2234 states that the board shall take action against any licensee who is charged with unprofessional conduct, which includes violating or attempting to violate any provision of this chapter. The original text ensures compliance, while the changed text only requires an attempt at compliance, thus directly contradicting the strict adherence expected by law. The law snippets provided clearly outline that professionals must not violate laws related to their practice. By using 'endeavor' and 'general accordance,' the contract suggests a lower standard of adherence than what is legally required, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Surgical agrees that it shall disclose promptly to Imprimis all inventions, ideas, concepts, and discoveries, including but not limited to processes, methods, formulas, biological materials, specimens, chemical compounds, formulations, software, data, techniques, products, applications, systems, procedures, technical information, drawings, reports and designs as well as improvements and modifications thereof and know-how thereto (whether or not protectable by copyright, patent, trademark, trade secret or any other proprietary rights), that it makes, conceives of, discovers or develops as a result of the receipt of Confidential Information (as defined in the Confidentiality Agreement referenced in Section 6 below) ("Work Product"). Surgical agrees that all Work Product shall be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis.
perturbation.changed_text: Surgical agrees that it shall endeavor to disclose to Imprimis significant inventions, ideas, concepts, and discoveries, limited to key processes and methods directly related to the Confidential Information received, that it explicitly makes, conceives of, discovers or develops as a direct and immediate result of the receipt of Confidential Information (as defined in the Confidentiality Agreement referenced in Section 6 below) ("Work Product"). Surgical agrees that all Work Product that is disclosed shall be the sole and exclusive property of Imprimis.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text introduces several ambiguities. Changing "shall disclose promptly" to "shall endeavor to disclose" weakens the obligation to disclose. Limiting the scope to "significant" inventions and "key processes and methods directly related" introduces subjectivity. The requirement that Work Product must be a "direct and immediate result" of confidential information creates a higher threshold for disclosure, potentially allowing Surgical to avoid disclosing relevant developments under a reasonable interpretation of "direct and immediate." This ambiguity creates legal uncertainty about what constitutes disclosable Work Product.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets from misappropriation. By narrowing the definition of Work Product that must be disclosed and weakening the obligation to disclose promptly, the modified text creates a loophole that could allow Surgical to misappropriate trade secrets without violating the contract. If an invention or discovery is deemed not "significant" or not a "direct and immediate result" of confidential information, Surgical might argue that it is not obligated to disclose it, thereby potentially misappropriating Imprimis's trade secrets.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the spirit of the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), as interpreted by the LLM's Law Explanation. While the scraped snippet only provides the title and citation of the act (CIV 3426), the Law Explanation posits that the changes weaken the obligation to disclose and narrow the definition of 'Work Product,' potentially allowing Surgical to misappropriate trade secrets without breaching the contract. By changing 'shall disclose promptly' to 'shall endeavor to disclose,' and limiting the scope of disclosable information, the modified text creates ambiguities and loopholes that could undermine the protections afforded by CUTSA. This directly relates to the Perturbation Explanation which states that this ambiguity creates legal uncertainty about what constitutes disclosable Work Product.

perturbation.original_text: The Sales Team shall use only such marketing and other materials for the Product in the Territory as are expressly approved in writing in advance by Imprimis, are consistent in all respects with the external marketing of such Product by Imprimis, include all warnings and instructions applicable for the proper use of the Product, comply with all applicable laws and regulations in the Territory, and do not contain any claims regarding a Product or its performance that the Sales Team does not reasonably demonstrate are supported by such Product or its performance.
perturbation.changed_text: The Sales Team should endeavor to use marketing and other materials for the Product in the Territory that are generally aligned with Imprimis' marketing strategies, including most warnings and instructions relevant to the Product's use, and comply with major applicable laws and regulations in the Territory, avoiding obviously unsupported claims regarding the Product's performance.
perturbation.explanation: This change significantly weakens the control Imprimis has over the Sales Team's marketing activities and introduces multiple ambiguities. Replacing "shall use only such...as are expressly approved in writing in advance" with "should endeavor to use...that are generally aligned" makes the use of approved materials optional and the alignment with Imprimis' strategies less strict. Replacing "include all warnings and instructions applicable for the proper use" with "including most warnings and instructions relevant to the Product's use" reduces the obligation to include all necessary warnings. Replacing "comply with all applicable laws and regulations" with "comply with major applicable laws and regulations" implies that some laws and regulations can be disregarded, creating a risk of non-compliance. Using "avoiding obviously unsupported claims" is highly subjective and leaves room for interpretation regarding what constitutes an "obviously" unsupported claim.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 21 U.S. Code § 352 - Misbranded drugs and devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 U.S. Code § 352 defines misbranding of drugs and devices, which includes false or misleading labeling, inadequate warnings, and failure to comply with FDA regulations. By weakening the requirements to use only approved marketing materials, include all necessary warnings, and comply with all applicable laws, the modified text increases the risk that the Sales Team will engage in misbranding. The Sales Team could use unapproved materials, omit necessary warnings, or make claims that are not fully supported, all of which would violate 21 U.S.C. § 352.

Misbranded drugs and devices A drug or device shall be deemed to be misbranded- (a) False or misleading label (1) If its labeling is false or misleading in any particular. Health care economic information provided to a payor, formulary committee, or other similar entity with knowledge and expertise in the area of health care economic analysis, carrying out its responsi
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that create a legal contradiction by weakening compliance with 21 U.S.C. § 352, regarding misbranded drugs and devices. The original text mandated strict adherence to approved marketing materials and all applicable laws. The modified text replaces "shall use only such...as are expressly approved in writing in advance" with a weaker "should endeavor to use...that are generally aligned." This shift reduces the obligation to use approved materials. Additionally, the change from "include all warnings and instructions applicable" to "including most warnings and instructions relevant" allows for the omission of necessary warnings, which directly contradicts the requirement against misleading labeling as stated in 21 USC 352 (a)(1): "If its labeling is false or misleading in any particular." Furthermore, the change from "comply with all applicable laws and regulations" to "comply with major applicable laws and regulations" creates a loophole, suggesting that some laws can be ignored, potentially leading to misbranding violations. The original contract provided a higher level of legal protection than the changed contract.
